SUMMARY:
MXenes possess numerous appealing characteristics, including high conductivity, significant specific area, and impressive stability . They also exhibit favorable electronic and optical properties. The fabrication of MXenes involves the selective removal of the A layer in the precursor phase.

### Journal of Materials Chemistry C

## Electronic properties and applications of MXenes: a theoretical review

![Check for updates](https://crossmark-cdn.crossref.org/widget/v2.0/logos/CROSSMARK_Color_horizontal.svg)

\* 

Corresponding authors

a

Computational Materials Science Research Team, RIKEN Advanced Institute for Computational Science (AICS), Kobe, Hyogo 650-0047, Japan
  
**E-mail:**
[khazaei@riken.jp](mailto:khazaei@riken.jp "khazaei@riken.jp")   
**Fax:** +81 78 304 4961

b

International Center for Materials Nanoarchitectonics, National Institute for Materials Science (NIMS), 1-1 Namiki, Tsukuba 305-0044, Ibaraki, Japan

c

Computational Condensed Matter Physics Laboratory, RIKEN, Wako, Saitama 351-0198, Japan

d

Computational Quantum Matter Research Team, RIKEN Center for Emergent Matter Science (CEMS), Wako, Saitama 351-0198, Japan

### Abstract

The recent chemical exfoliation of layered MAX phase compounds to novel two-dimensional transition metal carbides and nitrides, the so-called MXenes, has brought a new opportunity to materials science and technology. This review highlights the computational attempts that have been made to understand the physics and chemistry of this very promising family of advanced two-dimensional materials, and to exploit their novel and exceptional properties for electronic and energy harvesting applications.

![Graphical abstract: Electronic properties and applications of MXenes: a theoretical review](/en/Image/Get?imageInfo.ImageType=GA&imageInfo.ImageIdentifier.ManuscriptID=C7TC00140A&imageInfo.ImageIdentifier.Year=2017)![](https://www.rsc-cdn.org/pubs-core/2022.0.188/content/NewImages/Ajax-GA-Loader.gif)

enriched_points.expanded_content: MXenes are synthesized through the selective etching of the A-layer from MAX phases, resulting in a new class of 2D materials with unique properties.
These materials exhibit exceptional electrical conductivity, making them ideal for applications in electronics and energy storage, outperforming many traditional conductive materials.
MXenes possess variable surface terminations, allowing for customized surface chemistry that enhances their functionality in various applications, including catalysis and energy storage.
Due to their high conductivity and specific surface area, MXenes are effective in electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding, protecting sensitive electronic devices from interference.
MXenes demonstrate nonlinear optical properties, offering promising potential for advanced laser technologies, including photodetectors and optical switches in integrated photonics.
